questions.question: Differentiate between auto and static storage classes with respect to storage duration and default initial value.
keyPoints: Auto: automatic storage duration, created/destroyed with block entry/exit, initial value is garbage
Static: static storage duration, exists for entire program runtime, initialized to zero by default
Auto cannot retain value between function calls; static retains value

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'register' storage class? Explain its limitations.
keyPoints: Purpose: Request compiler to store variable in CPU register for faster access
Limitation: Cannot use address-of operator (&) on register variables
Limitation: Only applicable to automatic variables (local)
Compiler may ignore the request if insufficient registers available

questions.question: Explain the difference between scope, storage duration, and linkage in C.
keyPoints: Scope: Region of code where variable can be accessed (block, function, file)
Storage Duration: Time period during which variable exists in memory (automatic, static, allocated)
Linkage: Property determining whether references in different scopes refer to same entity (internal, external, none)

questions.question: What is the role of 'extern' keyword in C? How does it differ from 'static' when used with global variables?
keyPoints: extern declares a variable defined elsewhere, providing external linkage
static at file scope provides internal linkage - variable only visible in that file
extern variables can be shared across multiple source files
static variables restrict visibility to the defining file only

questions.question: Where are the following stored in memory: (i) Global variables (ii) Static local variables (iii) Auto local variables?
keyPoints: Global variables: Data segment (initialized) or BSS segment (uninitialized)
Static local variables: Data segment (initialized) or BSS segment (uninitialized)
Auto local variables: Stack memory
Dynamic memory (malloc): Heap memory

questions.question: Can a variable have both internal and external linkage simultaneously? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: No, linkage types are mutually exclusive
Internal linkage (static): identifier visible only within its source file
External linkage (extern): identifier visible across all source files
A variable can only have one type of linkage at a time
Output will be: 1 2 3
Static variable 'count' is initialized only once (at program startup)
Each function call increments the retained value, not reinitializes it
Demonstrates static storage duration - value persists between calls

questions.question: Write a C program to demonstrate all four storage classes (auto, register, static, extern). For each storage class, explain its characteristics including storage duration, scope, and default initial value.
keyPoints: auto: local variables, automatic duration, block scope, garbage initial value
register: local variables, automatic duration, block scope, cannot use &, garbage initial value
static (local): local scope but static duration, initialized once to zero, retains value
extern: file scope with external linkage, static duration, initialized to zero
Program should show variable modifications and persistence
Must include example of extern usage with multiple files or clear explanation

questions.question: Explain the memory layout of a C program. How do storage classes determine where variables are stored? Include a diagram showing the typical memory organization.
keyPoints: Memory segments: Code, Data (initialized globals), BSS (uninitialized globals), Heap, Stack
Auto/register: stored in stack, allocated/deallocated with function calls
Static (local and global): stored in data/BSS segment, allocated once at program startup
Global variables: stored in data/BSS segment with external/internal linkage
Dynamic memory: heap, managed by malloc/free
Each storage class maps to appropriate memory segment based on duration and scope

questions.question: Differentiate between: (i) Declaration vs Definition (ii) Internal vs External Linkage (iii) Automatic vs Static Storage Duration. Give suitable code examples for each.
keyPoints: Declaration: announces type, no storage allocation; Definition: allocates storage
Internal linkage: static at file scope, visible only in that file; External linkage: extern/default, visible across files
Automatic duration: created/destroyed with block (auto, register); Static duration: exists entire program (static, extern, global)
Must provide working code examples demonstrating each concept
First call output: a=1, s=6, x=11
Second call output: a=1, s=7, x=12
Third call output: a=1, s=8, x=13
'a' is auto: reinitialized to 0 each call, always prints 1
's' is static: initialized once, incremented each call
'x' is global (extern): exists throughout, incremented each call
Demonstrates automatic vs static vs global variable behavior
